narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings—pouring your heart into law school dreams only to have them paused like this feels deeply unfair, and I'm here to walk you through why our model saw your path to the bar exam the way it did, based on the details you shared. Picture your profile as a story the model read closely: it landed on a 46% chance of not passing the bar, enough to make us hesitate on admission. The biggest chapter pulling toward that tougher outcome was your Year 3 decile of 3.0, which stood out as the strongest signal, whispering that late-in-law-school performance might not build the momentum needed for bar success. Right behind it, your LSAT score of 25.5 echoed that concern, painting a picture of foundational challenges in the high-stakes reasoning the exam demands. Your Year 1 decile of 3.0 layered on more of the same, suggesting those early hurdles in school rankings set a pattern the model couldn't ignore. Then there's your background as a Black female from a middle-income family attending part-time—while none of these define you, the model saw them as threads weaving through data from past students, each gently tilting toward higher struggles on the bar. Even your solid undergraduate GPA of 3.4, which many would celebrate, got nudged into the mix as not quite lifting the other weights enough. It's this full mosaic—your deciles and LSAT carrying the heaviest load—that convinced the model your story might end in that 46% risk, despite your clear drive toward a legal career. This isn't about your worth; it's a data-driven snapshot urging us to protect spots for paths with brighter bar odds. If you strengthen those early signals—like boosting LSAT prep or early grades—you could rewrite this narrative for another try. You've got resilience in your story already; let's help you shape the next chapter. With empathy, Your Admissions Team
